skill.name: no-hardcoding
skill.description: Review code for hardcoded values and guide replacements with constants, environment variables, and configuration files.
skill.summary: Detects magic values and hardcoded secrets, then recommends safer constants and configuration patterns.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment access, credentials, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. AI review found these are documentation examples and grep review commands, not executable skill code or malicious instructions. The skill is publishable with low risk because it declares Bash and file editing tools for code review workflows.

low_findings.title: Bash Grep Review Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE low risk: the skill declares Bash access and documents grep commands for finding magic numbers, hardcoded URLs, and credential-like names. The commands are read-only searches over project files and do not show network transfer, deletion, or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly allows Bash and shows concrete grep commands. The semantic context is code review, and the commands are read-only patterns without user-controlled shell construction.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Access Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: fetch calls, process.env access, API key names, database URL names, and credential strings appear inside TypeScript examples that teach developers to avoid hardcoding. They are not executable skill implementation code and do not exfiltrate secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns are inside fenced documentation examples with BAD and GOOD labels. The surrounding text explains secure handling of URLs, credentials, and environment variables.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts do not correspond to executable cryptographic code or host enumeration behavior. The evidence is ordinary skill description text and constant names in examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation, DES usage, system probing command, or reconnaissance workflow. The flagged lines are metadata text and a DESKTOP breakpoint constant.

content.value_statement: Hardcoded values make changes risky and can expose secrets. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code find magic values and move them into safer constants or configuration.
actual_capabilities: Identifies magic numbers and repeated magic strings during code review.
Flags hardcoded URLs, paths, ports, timeouts, and delays.
Checks for credential-like values and recommends secret storage.
Recommends constants, enums, environment variables, and config files.
Provides a review checklist for hardcoding policy compliance.
Suggests project organization for constants and environment validation.
limitations: It relies on review patterns and may need project-specific exceptions.
It does not prove that every secret has been removed from history.
It may flag acceptable literals such as indexes or simple math values.
It does not replace dedicated secret scanning or static analysis tools.

use_cases.title: Pull Request Hardcoding Review
use_cases.description: Review changed files for magic values, repeated strings, hardcoded URLs, and exposed credentials before merge.

use_cases.title: Configuration Cleanup
use_cases.description: Move scattered values into constants, environment variables, and typed configuration modules.

use_cases.title: Secure Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: Add new features while keeping secrets, endpoints, and tunable limits outside business logic.

prompt_templates.title: Find Hardcoded Values
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one file or small change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for hardcoded values. List magic numbers, magic strings, URLs, paths, ports, and credentials. Suggest safer replacements.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor to Constants
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn repeated literals into named constants or enums.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the repeated literals in this module into constants or enums. Keep names clear and avoid changing behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design Environment Config
prompt_templates.scenario: Move runtime values out of source code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a configuration approach for this feature. Identify which values belong in environment variables, constants, or config files.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this pull request for hardcoding policy violations. Separate blockers, warnings, acceptable literals, and recommended follow-up changes.

output_examples.input: A review finds timeout values, status strings, and an API URL inside feature code.
output: Flags the timeout as a named duration constant.
Recommends an enum for repeated status values.
Moves the API base URL to validated environment configuration.

output_examples.input: A team asks whether numeric literals in array indexing should be removed.
output_examples.output: Explains that simple indexes, minus one checks, and clear math constants can be acceptable exceptions when their meaning is obvious.

output_examples.input: A new service needs database, API, and UI configuration values.
output: Classifies endpoint paths as API constants.
Classifies UI limits and breakpoints as typed constants.

best_practices: Name constants by business meaning, not by their current numeric value.
Validate required environment variables during application startup.
Document allowed literal exceptions so reviews stay consistent.
anti_patterns: Moving every literal into a constants file without useful names.
Storing secrets in source code, comments, examples, or test fixtures.
Using environment variables for values that are stable compile-time constants.

faq.question: Does this skill edit code automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide edits when the assistant has file tools, but the core purpose is review and refactoring guidance.

faq.question: Are all numbers considered hardcoded values?
faq.answer: No. Common indexes, simple math, and obvious values can be acceptable when they do not hide business meaning.

faq.question: Should URLs always use environment variables?
faq.answer: Base URLs and deployment-specific endpoints should use configuration. Stable route fragments can often be constants.

faq.question: Does this replace secret scanning?
faq.answer: No. Use dedicated secret scanning for repositories, history, and generated files.

faq.question: Can it help create an env validation file?
faq.answer: Yes. It can recommend required variables and a validation pattern for application startup.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
